**Session 2022-23**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 1/12)
chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill

**Written Evidence submitted by HOPE not hate (OSB113)**

**Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill**

**Key Points**

· HOPE not hate:

o Supports the principle of New Clause 7 and Amendments 48, 49, 76-93 in recognising the need of including characteristics as a determining factor of categorisation of platforms. However:

§ Schedule 11 1.4 (a) must be amended at a later stage to reflect the new condition of characteristics and also should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic.

§ The Government must outline what metrics and information they will use to make a determination of a platform based on their characteristics.

o Opposes amendments 6, 7 and 8, the removal of risk assessments and safety duties related to harmful content to adults.

o Supports the principle in NC3 of allowing content that breaks a platform’s Terms of Service to be removed but this must only occur with mandated risk assessments and minimum standards for platforms to follow.

o Opposes the use of user empowerment tools to reduce a user’s exposure to abuse or incitement to hatred against a protected characteristic to restrict the growth of online radicalisation.

§ Failing this, we support amendment 102 which would force platforms to have these tools ‘on’ by default.

o Supports Amendments 15a and 16a as an important extra tool to combat the rise of online radicalisation.

**Introduction**
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill

**Written Evidence submitted by HOPE not hate (OSB113)**

**Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill**

**Key Points**

· HOPE not hate:

o Supports the principle of New Clause 7 and Amendments 48, 49, 76-93 in recognising the need of including characteristics as a determining factor of categorisation of platforms. However:

§ Schedule 11 1.4 (a) must be amended at a later stage to reflect the new condition of characteristics and also should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic.

§ The Government must outline what metrics and information they will use to make a determination of a platform based on their characteristics.

o Opposes amendments 6, 7 and 8, the removal of risk assessments and safety duties related to harmful content to adults.

o Supports the principle in NC3 of allowing content that breaks a platform’s Terms of Service to be removed but this must only occur with mandated risk assessments and minimum standards for platforms to follow.

o Opposes the use of user empowerment tools to reduce a user’s exposure to abuse or incitement to hatred against a protected characteristic to restrict the growth of online radicalisation.

§ Failing this, we support amendment 102 which would force platforms to have these tools ‘on’ by default.

o Supports Amendments 15a and 16a as an important extra tool to combat the rise of online radicalisation.

**Introduction**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 2/12)
chunks.text: to hatred against a protected characteristic to restrict the growth of online radicalisation. § Failing this, we support amendment 102 which would force platforms to have these tools ‘on’ by default. o Supports Amendments 15a and 16a as an important extra tool to combat the rise of online radicalisation. **Introduction** 1.HOPE not hate is the UK’s leading anti-fascist campaign group, tracking, analysing and defeating the far right in the UK and campaign on the issues that give rise to them. 2.Since 2004, HOPE not hate have successfully combatted the far right at the ballot box, in communities and ever-increasingly online. To understand and infiltrate the far right, we used to have to turn up in the back rooms of pubs to find far right meetings. Now, almost exclusively, this involves tracking groups, networks and individuals online and on a vast range of social media platforms. 3.Despite ten years of attempts by social media companies to regulate and moderate hate speech, it is still rife across every major social media platform and on many smaller platforms too. Many platforms have also allowed or welcomed the far right to flourish on their platforms, spreading their messages of hate and recruiting and organising supporters. 4.Because of this, HOPE not hate has been supportive of the principle of legislation to tackle Online Harms since its introduction at draft stage into Parliament and has produced multiple reports and briefings related to the Online Safety Bill \[1\] \[2\] \[3\] \[4\] \[5\] \[6\]. 5.HOPE not hate Political Organiser Liron Velleman also gave Oral Evidence at the Third Sitting of the Online Safety Bill Public Bill Committee \[7\]. **Response to amendments tabled** NC7, AMENDMENTS 48, 49, 76-93
chunks.display_text: 1.HOPE not hate is the UK’s leading anti-fascist campaign group, tracking, analysing and defeating the far right in the UK and campaign on the issues that give rise to them. 2.Since 2004, HOPE not hate have successfully combatted the far right at the ballot box, in communities and ever-increasingly online. To understand and infiltrate the far right, we used to have to turn up in the back rooms of pubs to find far right meetings. Now, almost exclusively, this involves tracking groups, networks and individuals online and on a vast range of social media platforms. 3.Despite ten years of attempts by social media companies to regulate and moderate hate speech, it is still rife across every major social media platform and on many smaller platforms too. Many platforms have also allowed or welcomed the far right to flourish on their platforms, spreading their messages of hate and recruiting and organising supporters. 4.Because of this, HOPE not hate has been supportive of the principle of legislation to tackle Online Harms since its introduction at draft stage into Parliament and has produced multiple reports and briefings related to the Online Safety Bill \[1\] \[2\] \[3\] \[4\] \[5\] \[6\]. 5.HOPE not hate Political Organiser Liron Velleman also gave Oral Evidence at the Third Sitting of the Online Safety Bill Public Bill Committee \[7\]. **Response to amendments tabled** NC7, AMENDMENTS 48, 49, 76-93

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 3/12)
chunks.text: produced multiple reports and briefings related to the Online Safety Bill \[1\] \[2\] \[3\] \[4\] \[5\] \[6\]. 5.HOPE not hate Political Organiser Liron Velleman also gave Oral Evidence at the Third Sitting of the Online Safety Bill Public Bill Committee \[7\]. **Response to amendments tabled** NC7, AMENDMENTS 48, 49, 76-93 6.HOPE not hate have consistently raised the issue of small size, high harm platforms not being included as Category 1 platforms. In response to the Government statement on the legislation in December 2020, our Director of Research Dr Joe Mulhall said: "We are especially concerned about the issue of smaller or bespoke platforms. The government talked a lot today about cooperating with platforms to remove hateful content. But there are platforms which proactively seek to give voice to the forces of hate, designing their platform to avoid regulation and action by authorities. We need to see more detail on how those smaller platforms will be regulated so those who seek to allow extremists to radicalise and recruit are tackled too." 7.In our evidence to the Pre-Legislative Scrutiny Committee for the Online Safety Bill, we raised the question of whether the Bill adequately dealt with small but extreme platforms, explaining that "some of the most dangerous platforms used by the far right to spread hate and organise are smaller platforms they have co-opted, or even small platforms they create themselves." \[8\] 8.In the same evidence, we also highlighted that the far right use numerous social media platforms simultaneously, using major platforms for their moderate content designed to propagandise and recruit but also using smaller and more laxly moderated platforms to organise and share more extreme content designed to radicalise.
chunks.display_text: 6.HOPE not hate have consistently raised the issue of small size, high harm platforms not being included as Category 1 platforms. In response to the Government statement on the legislation in December 2020, our Director of Research Dr Joe Mulhall said: "We are especially concerned about the issue of smaller or bespoke platforms. The government talked a lot today about cooperating with platforms to remove hateful content. But there are platforms which proactively seek to give voice to the forces of hate, designing their platform to avoid regulation and action by authorities. We need to see more detail on how those smaller platforms will be regulated so those who seek to allow extremists to radicalise and recruit are tackled too." 7.In our evidence to the Pre-Legislative Scrutiny Committee for the Online Safety Bill, we raised the question of whether the Bill adequately dealt with small but extreme platforms, explaining that "some of the most dangerous platforms used by the far right to spread hate and organise are smaller platforms they have co-opted, or even small platforms they create themselves." \[8\] 8.In the same evidence, we also highlighted that the far right use numerous social media platforms simultaneously, using major platforms for their moderate content designed to propagandise and recruit but also using smaller and more laxly moderated platforms to organise and share more extreme content designed to radicalise.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 4/12)
chunks.text: themselves." \[8\] 8.In the same evidence, we also highlighted that the far right use numerous social media platforms simultaneously, using major platforms for their moderate content designed to propagandise and recruit but also using smaller and more laxly moderated platforms to organise and share more extreme content designed to radicalise. 9.Therefore, we called for the definition of Category 1 services to look beyond the size of a platform and should also explore the type of harmful content and the user base of the platform. 10.The social media landscape has changed fundamentally in recent years and a huge amount of the most harmful behaviour happens on small and alt-tech platforms as the far right and other hateful actors have begun to migrate to alternative and usually smaller platforms. 11.These platforms are either co-opted platforms which are not created for, or by the far right, but have been widely used by their networks and groups due to loose policies, a lack of moderation or a lax attitude to content removal or bespoke platforms which are created by the far right themselves or by those specifically courting extremists. Many of these platforms have strong functionality but little to no moderation. 12.This situation has only worsened as far right groups and networks develop both their technological capabilities and their organising methods. Our experience of tracking the far right throughout the Covid-19 pandemic and as we emerge from it has shown the proliferation of both harms and users on platforms unlikely to currently be categorised as Category 1 by the current version of the Bill. 13.At Report Stage, HOPE not hate supported cross-party efforts to amend Schedule 11 to include ‘characteristics’ of a platform in determining its categorisation, which we believe would bring low user, high harm platforms into Category 1.
chunks.display_text: 9.Therefore, we called for the definition of Category 1 services to look beyond the size of a platform and should also explore the type of harmful content and the user base of the platform. 10.The social media landscape has changed fundamentally in recent years and a huge amount of the most harmful behaviour happens on small and alt-tech platforms as the far right and other hateful actors have begun to migrate to alternative and usually smaller platforms. 11.These platforms are either co-opted platforms which are not created for, or by the far right, but have been widely used by their networks and groups due to loose policies, a lack of moderation or a lax attitude to content removal or bespoke platforms which are created by the far right themselves or by those specifically courting extremists. Many of these platforms have strong functionality but little to no moderation. 12.This situation has only worsened as far right groups and networks develop both their technological capabilities and their organising methods. Our experience of tracking the far right throughout the Covid-19 pandemic and as we emerge from it has shown the proliferation of both harms and users on platforms unlikely to currently be categorised as Category 1 by the current version of the Bill. 13.At Report Stage, HOPE not hate supported cross-party efforts to amend Schedule 11 to include ‘characteristics’ of a platform in determining its categorisation, which we believe would bring low user, high harm platforms into Category 1.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 5/12)
chunks.text: to currently be categorised as Category 1 by the current version of the Bill. 13.At Report Stage, HOPE not hate supported cross-party efforts to amend Schedule 11 to include ‘characteristics’ of a platform in determining its categorisation, which we believe would bring low user, high harm platforms into Category 1. 14.We felt this was the best solution as it would give a legislative footing for OFCOM to include these platforms from the outset in their first threshold conditions after Royal Assent and would give this power to the regulator who will be supervising platforms. 15.Amendments 76-79, 83, 84 and 86-91 are a recognition from the Government that after years of campaigning and lobbying by HOPE not hate and others, the categorisation system must change to reflect the issue of low user, high harm platforms. 16.HOPE not hate supports the intention of these amendments and believes they represent a significant step forward in curbing the outputs of far right networks, groups and individuals. 17.However, these amendments still leave us with some questions about the system and would want to seek clarification and possibly some changes to these amendments at future stages of the Bill if they were to be supported by the Public General Committee. a.The Government haven’t sought to amend Schedule 11 1.4 (a) which requires a condition on size and on functionality to be met. This must be amended at Report Stage to reflect the new condition of characteristics and also should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic for meeting threshold conditions. **Will the Government be bringing forward amendments to reflect characteristics and ensure they on their own would be sufficient to meet the conditions at a future occasion?**
chunks.display_text: 14.We felt this was the best solution as it would give a legislative footing for OFCOM to include these platforms from the outset in their first threshold conditions after Royal Assent and would give this power to the regulator who will be supervising platforms. 15.Amendments 76-79, 83, 84 and 86-91 are a recognition from the Government that after years of campaigning and lobbying by HOPE not hate and others, the categorisation system must change to reflect the issue of low user, high harm platforms. 16.HOPE not hate supports the intention of these amendments and believes they represent a significant step forward in curbing the outputs of far right networks, groups and individuals. 17.However, these amendments still leave us with some questions about the system and would want to seek clarification and possibly some changes to these amendments at future stages of the Bill if they were to be supported by the Public General Committee. a.The Government haven’t sought to amend Schedule 11 1.4 (a) which requires a condition on size and on functionality to be met. This must be amended at Report Stage to reflect the new condition of characteristics and also should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic for meeting threshold conditions. **Will the Government be bringing forward amendments to reflect characteristics and ensure they on their own would be sufficient to meet the conditions at a future occasion?**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 6/12)
chunks.text: the new condition of characteristics and also should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic for meeting threshold conditions. **Will the Government be bringing forward amendments to reflect characteristics and ensure they on their own would be sufficient to meet the conditions at a future occasion?** b.Whilst the determination of thresholds on size will be formed using a specified number, the characteristics of the platform will also need to be determined. **What information, metrics and sources will the Secretary of State use to determine threshold conditions for characteristics?** As the characteristics of a platform would be based on extensive research, we believe OFCOM would be better placed to make this determination. c.New Clause 7 adds provisions in the Bill for a list of emerging platforms to be assessed by OFCOM. The current clause requires conditions on size and characteristics for a platform to be added to this list. This should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic. **Will the Government be bringing forward amendments to ensure characteristics would be sufficient on their own to meet threshold conditions? What information, metrics and sources will the Secretary of State use to determine characteristics that meet an emerging list of platforms?** NC3, NC4, AMENDMENTS 6-75 18.One of the reasons HOPE not hate has long supported legislation to tackle online harms is because of the range of terrorist, illegal, abusive and harmful content that can be found on a large number of social media platforms on a regular basis, viewed by children and adults alike. 19.We have also relentlessly highlighted the need and campaigned for social media companies to tackle this range of content found on their platforms with varying degrees of success.
chunks.display_text: b.Whilst the determination of thresholds on size will be formed using a specified number, the characteristics of the platform will also need to be determined. **What information, metrics and sources will the Secretary of State use to determine threshold conditions for characteristics?** As the characteristics of a platform would be based on extensive research, we believe OFCOM would be better placed to make this determination. c.New Clause 7 adds provisions in the Bill for a list of emerging platforms to be assessed by OFCOM. The current clause requires conditions on size and characteristics for a platform to be added to this list. This should be amended to require a condition of either size or characteristic. **Will the Government be bringing forward amendments to ensure characteristics would be sufficient on their own to meet threshold conditions? What information, metrics and sources will the Secretary of State use to determine characteristics that meet an emerging list of platforms?** NC3, NC4, AMENDMENTS 6-75 18.One of the reasons HOPE not hate has long supported legislation to tackle online harms is because of the range of terrorist, illegal, abusive and harmful content that can be found on a large number of social media platforms on a regular basis, viewed by children and adults alike. 19.We have also relentlessly highlighted the need and campaigned for social media companies to tackle this range of content found on their platforms with varying degrees of success.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 7/12)
chunks.text: that can be found on a large number of social media platforms on a regular basis, viewed by children and adults alike. 19.We have also relentlessly highlighted the need and campaigned for social media companies to tackle this range of content found on their platforms with varying degrees of success. 20.It is clear that illegal content of any sort has no place on social media platforms and we welcome any attempts to force platforms to comply with this basic duty that many have failed on for so many years. 21.However, tackling illegal content alone won’t solve the issue that requires this legislative intervention. Steps must be taken to seek to reduce harm and abuse on social media platforms, preserving freedom from abuse and harassment, and protect vulnerable groups who are targets of online hate. We must end up with a form of regulation of platforms that places democratic rights front-and-centre. 22.It is commonly accepted, including by the Government in these amendments, that other content causes harm on the platform and must be addressed. 23.However, the Government amendments weaken the legislative footing for Parliament and OFCOM to influence companies to make system and design changes to tackle these harms. 24.In 2021, HOPE not hate led a collaboration with Reset:Tech, the Antisemitism Policy Trust, IMPRESS, Glitch, Clean up the Internet, Demos and Catch 22 on a report to make the case that ‘legal but harmful should continue to be included in the Online Safety Bill’.\[9\] 25.This report not only outlined the reasons why tackling harmful content to adults would enable much greater freedom of speech on social media platforms, it also provided case studies of areas of content that would fall into this category.
chunks.display_text: 20.It is clear that illegal content of any sort has no place on social media platforms and we welcome any attempts to force platforms to comply with this basic duty that many have failed on for so many years. 21.However, tackling illegal content alone won’t solve the issue that requires this legislative intervention. Steps must be taken to seek to reduce harm and abuse on social media platforms, preserving freedom from abuse and harassment, and protect vulnerable groups who are targets of online hate. We must end up with a form of regulation of platforms that places democratic rights front-and-centre. 22.It is commonly accepted, including by the Government in these amendments, that other content causes harm on the platform and must be addressed. 23.However, the Government amendments weaken the legislative footing for Parliament and OFCOM to influence companies to make system and design changes to tackle these harms. 24.In 2021, HOPE not hate led a collaboration with Reset:Tech, the Antisemitism Policy Trust, IMPRESS, Glitch, Clean up the Internet, Demos and Catch 22 on a report to make the case that ‘legal but harmful should continue to be included in the Online Safety Bill’.\[9\] 25.This report not only outlined the reasons why tackling harmful content to adults would enable much greater freedom of speech on social media platforms, it also provided case studies of areas of content that would fall into this category.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 8/12)
chunks.text: harmful should continue to be included in the Online Safety Bill’.\[9\] 25.This report not only outlined the reasons why tackling harmful content to adults would enable much greater freedom of speech on social media platforms, it also provided case studies of areas of content that would fall into this category. 26.Some of the case studies included: Covid-19 and other health misinformation, antisemitic, racist and misogynist abuse, disinformation, abuse of public figures, conspiracy theories and Holocaust denial. 27.These case studies are regularly found on social media platforms, are hugely damaging and have no place in the public marketplace of ideas and conversation. These case studies and more inform our position on the need for harmful content to adults to remain part of the Bill. 28.In addition, to tackle any content that harms adults, we recognise the need to take a variety of measures into consideration depending on the severity, and that platforms should be mandated to assess the risks of these in order to best serve their users. 29.The Government have sought to mitigate these changes with a so-called ‘triple shield’ of protection. 30.On the first ‘shield’, we support any efforts to remove illegal content and this should occur, however this should be an absolute minimum requirement and does not tackle huge swathes of content that required this legislation in the first place. 31.On the second ‘shield’, we support the ability of social media companies to be able to remove, and otherwise diminish the impact of, content that breaches their terms of service. Nevertheless, this ‘shield’ risks being rendered irrelevant without any minimum standards or even risk assessments for the impact of particular types of content on their platforms.
chunks.display_text: 26.Some of the case studies included: Covid-19 and other health misinformation, antisemitic, racist and misogynist abuse, disinformation, abuse of public figures, conspiracy theories and Holocaust denial. 27.These case studies are regularly found on social media platforms, are hugely damaging and have no place in the public marketplace of ideas and conversation. These case studies and more inform our position on the need for harmful content to adults to remain part of the Bill. 28.In addition, to tackle any content that harms adults, we recognise the need to take a variety of measures into consideration depending on the severity, and that platforms should be mandated to assess the risks of these in order to best serve their users. 29.The Government have sought to mitigate these changes with a so-called ‘triple shield’ of protection. 30.On the first ‘shield’, we support any efforts to remove illegal content and this should occur, however this should be an absolute minimum requirement and does not tackle huge swathes of content that required this legislation in the first place. 31.On the second ‘shield’, we support the ability of social media companies to be able to remove, and otherwise diminish the impact of, content that breaches their terms of service. Nevertheless, this ‘shield’ risks being rendered irrelevant without any minimum standards or even risk assessments for the impact of particular types of content on their platforms.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 9/12)
chunks.text: the ability of social media companies to be able to remove, and otherwise diminish the impact of, content that breaches their terms of service. Nevertheless, this ‘shield’ risks being rendered irrelevant without any minimum standards or even risk assessments for the impact of particular types of content on their platforms. 32.Under the new proposals, companies would be incentivised to reduce the reach of their terms of service to not fall foul of the stringent measures in NC3 or amend them in such a way that continues their current practices of removing content on their platform that they already do, leaving swathes of hate, disinformation and abuse on platforms. 33.One only has to look at the new leadership of Twitter to see the ability of social media platforms to vastly amend their terms of service to allow content such as Covid misinformation on their platforms. 34.Additionally, there are platforms that would likely fit under Category 1 according to new Government amendments which are either run by far right individuals or specifically court their hateful extremism. 35.These platforms have allowed, and on some occasions encouraged, content that would be expected to fall under any mainstream platforms’ terms of service to exist on their platforms and this ‘shield’ would do nothing to curb this. 36.Finally, the third ‘shield’ in NC4 allows users to have greater control over the content they see, but this fundamentally misunderstands the issue of this content reaching any users in the first place.
chunks.display_text: 32.Under the new proposals, companies would be incentivised to reduce the reach of their terms of service to not fall foul of the stringent measures in NC3 or amend them in such a way that continues their current practices of removing content on their platform that they already do, leaving swathes of hate, disinformation and abuse on platforms. 33.One only has to look at the new leadership of Twitter to see the ability of social media platforms to vastly amend their terms of service to allow content such as Covid misinformation on their platforms. 34.Additionally, there are platforms that would likely fit under Category 1 according to new Government amendments which are either run by far right individuals or specifically court their hateful extremism. 35.These platforms have allowed, and on some occasions encouraged, content that would be expected to fall under any mainstream platforms’ terms of service to exist on their platforms and this ‘shield’ would do nothing to curb this. 36.Finally, the third ‘shield’ in NC4 allows users to have greater control over the content they see, but this fundamentally misunderstands the issue of this content reaching any users in the first place.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 10/12)
chunks.text: terms of service to exist on their platforms and this ‘shield’ would do nothing to curb this. 36.Finally, the third ‘shield’ in NC4 allows users to have greater control over the content they see, but this fundamentally misunderstands the issue of this content reaching any users in the first place. 37.Content such as racist abuse, misinformation and hatred has a detrimental impact on minoritised communities on social media and users from those communities should not be subjected to it. The continued spreading of this has resulted in many users no longer feeling that social media is a safe place for them to exist, worsening the diversity of thought and opinion on platforms. 38.This content remaining on platforms for users choosing not to use the user empowerment tools risks the radicalisation of users and the growth of hateful extremism on platforms. 39.To use a recent case study, HOPE not hate uncovered the history of an individual sharing conspiracy material, anti-Muslim content around ‘grooming gangs’ and consumed material from far right groups and individuals including Traditional Britain Group, Stephen Lennon (Tommy Robinson) and the National Housing Party UK. Most of this content was not illegal. 40.On the 30th October 2022, this individual tweeted *‘Your children will feel the pain we will obliterate them Muslim children are now our target And there disgusting women will be targeted mothers and sisters Is burn alive’*. \[sic\] 41.An hour later, the individual, later named by Kent Police as Andrew Leak, attacked a migrant processing centre in Dover with two or three petrol bombs. 42.This is the real world consequence of allowing hateful content on platforms and it should not be up to users to decide how much racism and other forms of hatred they see on their platforms; it shouldn’t be there at all.
chunks.display_text: 37.Content such as racist abuse, misinformation and hatred has a detrimental impact on minoritised communities on social media and users from those communities should not be subjected to it. The continued spreading of this has resulted in many users no longer feeling that social media is a safe place for them to exist, worsening the diversity of thought and opinion on platforms. 38.This content remaining on platforms for users choosing not to use the user empowerment tools risks the radicalisation of users and the growth of hateful extremism on platforms. 39.To use a recent case study, HOPE not hate uncovered the history of an individual sharing conspiracy material, anti-Muslim content around ‘grooming gangs’ and consumed material from far right groups and individuals including Traditional Britain Group, Stephen Lennon (Tommy Robinson) and the National Housing Party UK. Most of this content was not illegal. 40.On the 30th October 2022, this individual tweeted *‘Your children will feel the pain we will obliterate them Muslim children are now our target And there disgusting women will be targeted mothers and sisters Is burn alive’*. \[sic\] 41.An hour later, the individual, later named by Kent Police as Andrew Leak, attacked a migrant processing centre in Dover with two or three petrol bombs. 42.This is the real world consequence of allowing hateful content on platforms and it should not be up to users to decide how much racism and other forms of hatred they see on their platforms; it shouldn’t be there at all.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 11/12)
chunks.text: centre in Dover with two or three petrol bombs. 42.This is the real world consequence of allowing hateful content on platforms and it should not be up to users to decide how much racism and other forms of hatred they see on their platforms; it shouldn’t be there at all. 43.**Therefore, on these clauses, HOPE not hate:** a.**Opposes amendments 6, 7 and 8, the removal of risk assessments and safety duties related to harmful content to adults.** b.**Supports the principle in NC3 of allowing content that breaks a platform’s Terms of Service to be removed but this must only occur with mandated risk assessments and minimum standards for platforms to follow.** c.**Opposes the use of user empowerment tools to reduce a user’s exposure to abuse or incitement to hatred against a protected characteristic to restrict the growth of online radicalisation.** i.**Failing this, we support amendment 102 which would force platforms to have these tools ‘on’ by default.** AMENDMENTS 15a and 16a 44.Much of the legal content that HOPE not hate tracks online is not individual abuse from user to user but is more likely to be content that could be broadly categorised as ‘hateful extremism’. 45.These types of content are used by the far right to spread their hatred, recruit new supporters and organise tactics up to and including terrorism. 46.In recent years, users (such as the case study in points 39-42 of this submission) who end up committing offences either in posting illegal content relating to hate speech or terrorist content, or committing hate crimes or terrorist acts in person have often begun their radicalisation journey by consuming legal forms of hateful extremism online.
chunks.display_text: 43.**Therefore, on these clauses, HOPE not hate:** a.**Opposes amendments 6, 7 and 8, the removal of risk assessments and safety duties related to harmful content to adults.** b.**Supports the principle in NC3 of allowing content that breaks a platform’s Terms of Service to be removed but this must only occur with mandated risk assessments and minimum standards for platforms to follow.** c.**Opposes the use of user empowerment tools to reduce a user’s exposure to abuse or incitement to hatred against a protected characteristic to restrict the growth of online radicalisation.** i.**Failing this, we support amendment 102 which would force platforms to have these tools ‘on’ by default.** AMENDMENTS 15a and 16a 44.Much of the legal content that HOPE not hate tracks online is not individual abuse from user to user but is more likely to be content that could be broadly categorised as ‘hateful extremism’. 45.These types of content are used by the far right to spread their hatred, recruit new supporters and organise tactics up to and including terrorism. 46.In recent years, users (such as the case study in points 39-42 of this submission) who end up committing offences either in posting illegal content relating to hate speech or terrorist content, or committing hate crimes or terrorist acts in person have often begun their radicalisation journey by consuming legal forms of hateful extremism online.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 12/12)
chunks.text: as the case study in points 39-42 of this submission) who end up committing offences either in posting illegal content relating to hate speech or terrorist content, or committing hate crimes or terrorist acts in person have often begun their radicalisation journey by consuming legal forms of hateful extremism online. 47.The current list of content in Amendment 15 is not sufficient to cover much of this content as it relies on the content being abusive or inciting hatred against an individual based on their protected characteristics. This ignores the societal harms that far right extremist content can sometimes create that does not seek to target an individual or a protected characteristic. 48.For example, users posting conspiracy theories around the ‘New World Order’, ‘globalists’ or ‘QAnon’ misinform other users and can lead to their radicalisation, even with the user empowerment tools from this legislation turned on as they would not fit the current lists of content in Amendment 15. 49.**HOPE not hate supports these amendments as an extra tool to combat the rise of online radicalisation.** *December 2022* --- \[1\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/A-Better-Web-Final-.pdf \[2\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/2020/12/15/online-harms-a-step-in-the-right-direction/ \[3\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/2021/06/07/hope-not-hates-response-to-the-draft-online-safety-bill/ \[4\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Free-Speech-For-All-2021-08-v21Oct.pdf \[5\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Online-Safety-Bill-2021-09-v1-2.pdf \[6\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/Online-Safety-Bill-Report-Stage.pdf \[7\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/commons/2022-05-26/debates/a8f25ba3-fcfa-460b-8287-055606dcc344/OnlineSafetyBill(ThirdSitting)#contribution-DCD80454-1691-4A1D-A931-1EBB268E2E5F) \[8\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Online-Safety-Bill-2021-09-v1-2.pdf \[8\] \[9\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Free-Speech-For-All-2021-08-v21Oct.pdf \[9\]
chunks.display_text: 47.The current list of content in Amendment 15 is not sufficient to cover much of this content as it relies on the content being abusive or inciting hatred against an individual based on their protected characteristics. This ignores the societal harms that far right extremist content can sometimes create that does not seek to target an individual or a protected characteristic. 48.For example, users posting conspiracy theories around the ‘New World Order’, ‘globalists’ or ‘QAnon’ misinform other users and can lead to their radicalisation, even with the user empowerment tools from this legislation turned on as they would not fit the current lists of content in Amendment 15. 49.**HOPE not hate supports these amendments as an extra tool to combat the rise of online radicalisation.** *December 2022* --- \[1\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/A-Better-Web-Final-.pdf \[2\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/2020/12/15/online-harms-a-step-in-the-right-direction/ \[3\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/2021/06/07/hope-not-hates-response-to-the-draft-online-safety-bill/ \[4\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Free-Speech-For-All-2021-08-v21Oct.pdf \[5\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Online-Safety-Bill-2021-09-v1-2.pdf \[6\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/Online-Safety-Bill-Report-Stage.pdf \[7\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/commons/2022-05-26/debates/a8f25ba3-fcfa-460b-8287-055606dcc344/OnlineSafetyBill(ThirdSitting)#contribution-DCD80454-1691-4A1D-A931-1EBB268E2E5F) \[8\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Online-Safety-Bill-2021-09-v1-2.pdf \[8\] \[9\] https://hopenothate.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Free-Speech-For-All-2021-08-v21Oct.pdf \[9\]
